summary:
The Russo-Swedish Wars encompassed several conflicts between Russia and Sweden for Baltic dominance, most significantly the [[event:2348|Great Northern War]] (1700–1721) and the later wars of 1741–1743 (the Russo-Swedish War or 'War of the Hats') and 1788–1790 (the Russo-Swedish War under Gustav III). The most consequential was the Great Northern War, in which Russia under [[event:2349|Peter the Great]] defeated Sweden under Charles XII, destroying Swedish hegemony in the Baltic and establishing Russia as a major European power. The subsequent smaller wars represented Swedish attempts to recover lost territories — both failed, with the 1743 Treaty of Åbo further reducing Swedish territory and the 1790 Treaty of Värälä essentially confirming the status quo.

The pattern of these wars reflects a broader historical shift: Sweden, which had been the dominant Baltic power in the 17th century (the 'Age of Greatness' or Stormaktstiden), was progressively reduced to a secondary power as Russia expanded westward under Peter the Great and his successors. Sweden ceded its Baltic provinces (Livonia, Estonia, Ingria, and part of Karelia) to Russia in the 1721 Treaty of Nystad, completing the transformation of the Baltic into a Russian sphere of influence. The 1788–1790 war, initiated by King Gustav III in a constitutional crisis context, was notable for the Swedish naval victory at the Second Battle of Svensksund (1790) — Sweden's greatest naval victory — before the peace of Värälä returned to the pre-war boundaries. Sweden subsequently reoriented its foreign policy toward neutrality and non-alignment, a stance that would persist for over two centuries.

Battle Svensksund 1790 Swedish naval victory Gustav III
